allergens: gluten (possible from fish sauce or batter)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of spicy tom yum broth with crispy fried fish pieces served alongside or over steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — cooked white rice (NDB 20444), fried fish (NDB 15264), and Thai tom yum soup base; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Tom Yum Crispy Fish Soup with Rice exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 45% steamed jasmine rice, 25% crispy fried fish, 30% tom yum broth with vegetables. USDA SR Legacy used for rice and fried fish macros; FAO/INFOODS SEA used for Thai soup base reference. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce content typical of tom yum broth.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled) GI reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Crispy Fish Soup with Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant glycemic contributor being steamed white rice (GI 64–72 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The overall dish GI is moderated downward from plain rice GI due to the presence of protein from crispy fish, fat from frying oil, and the acidic tom yum broth (lime juice, lemongrass), all of which slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 65.25) / 100 ≈ 36.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 14.5 × 4.5 = 65.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and tom yum broth
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving delivers approximately 1890 mg sodium, representing 82–94% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, limit broth consumption, and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from steamed white rice as the primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 36.5 per serving is moderate-to-high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion, substituting brown rice if available, and pairing the meal with the protein-rich crispy fish to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from deep-frying, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1890 mg per serving) poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, while the deep-fried fish contributes moderate saturated and total fat; however, the fish protein and potential omega-3 content offer partial cardiovascular benefit, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and broth intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Crispy Fish Soup with Rice (Thai: ต้มยำปลากรอบข้าว, romanized: Tom Yam Pla Krob Khao) is a Thai dish consisting of deep-fried crispy fish pieces served in a hot and sour tom yum broth, accompanied by steamed jasmine rice. The tom yum broth is prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai bird's eye chillies, lime juice, fish sauce, mushrooms, and tomatoes to produce a characteristically spicy, sour, and aromatic soup. The fish — typically a white-fleshed variety — is battered or coated and deep-fried until golden and crunchy, then placed in or alongside the broth at serving time to preserve its crispy texture. The dish delivers a complex interplay of textures: crunchy fish, soft rice, and a fragrant liquid broth. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 531 kcal, 32 g protein, 17 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate, with sodium content around 1890 mg per serving due to fish sauce. The dish is a good source of lean fish protein and contains beneficial micronutrients including phosphorus, potassium, and vitamin C from the lime and chilli components. Clinically, the high sodium load is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, while the moderate glycemic load from white rice warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is popular in Thai restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia, often listed on menus as a comfort meal option.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Tom Yum Crispy Fish Soup with Rice exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for fried fish and cooked white rice, supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA references for Thai soup base. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor (white rice) with documented moderation factors. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved source entry covers this exact composite dish. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce typical in tom yum broth — approximately 420 mg per 100 g across the full dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — deep-frying in vegetable oil at restaurant level is not expected to produce significant trans fat; confirmed negligible per USDA frying oil references). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source analysis. GI set to 56 (medium) as an analogous estimate based on white rice GI moderated by protein, fat, and acid components per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (56 × 65.25) / 100 ≈ 36.5 for a 450 g serving containing approximately 65.25 g carbohydrate. Restaurant source: Saap Saap Thai — Funan, Singapore (field survey data listing, food_id GD001561). This dish is a Thai restaurant menu item sold in Singapore; it is not a traditional street food with a standardised recipe, so nutrient values may vary by restaurant preparation.
